message.abstract:
<jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Certain serum proteins, including CRP and D-dimer, have prognostic value in patients with SARS-CoV-2 infection. Nonetheless, these factors are non-specific, and provide limited mechanistic insight into the peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) populations which drive the pathogenesis of severe COVID-19. To identify novel cellular phenotypes associated with disease progression, we here describe a comprehensive, unbiased analysis of the total and plasma membrane proteomes of PBMCs from a cohort of 40 unvaccinated individuals with SARS-CoV-2 infection, spanning the whole spectrum of disease severity. Combined with RNA-seq and flow cytometry data from the same donors, we define a comprehensive multi-omic profile for each severity level, revealing cumulative immune cell dysregulation in progressive disease. In particular, the cell surface proteins CEACAMs1, 6 and 8, CD177, CD63 and CD89 are strongly associated with severe COVID-19, corresponding to the emergence of atypical CD3
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD177
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and CD16
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CEACAM1/6/8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  mononuclear cells. Utilisation of these markers may facilitate real-time patient assessment by flow cytometry, and identify immune cell populations that could be targeted to ameliorate immunopathology.
                </jats:p>
title: Proteomic analysis of circulating immune cells identifies novel cellular phenotypes associated with COVID-19 severity

author.name: Cambridge Institute of Therapeutic Immunology and Infectious Disease-National Institute of Health Research (CITIID-NIHR) COVID BioResource Collaboration
